name: Plugin Architecture

forces: Extensibility ↔ stability of the host contract
x.structure: A minimal core (microkernel) defines extension points / interfaces; independent plugins implement them and are discovered and loaded (often dynamically) at runtime.
antiPatterns: Leaky plugin API exposing core internals
x.scale: Application architecture; editors, IDEs, browsers, CMSs, DAWs.

def: An architecture with a small stable core that exposes extension points, letting independent plugins add capability without modifying the core.
problem: Hard-coding every feature into the core makes it bloated and impossible for third parties to extend safely.
mechanism: The core defines plugin interfaces and a discovery/loading mechanism (registry, dynamic libraries, manifests). Plugins implement the contract and are loaded at startup or on demand. The contract — not the implementation — is the dependency.
limits: Stable, well-versioned contract is hard to design
failures: Breaking the plugin API between versions
Plugins reaching into core internals (leaky contract)
whenNot: Fixed feature set with no extension need
myths: "Plugins are always safe to load" — without sandboxing they run with full host privilege
